Row1.Pathway ID/Name: Canonical EGFR mutant signaling (Ex19del/L858R) via RAS–RAF–MEK–ERK, PI3K–AKT–mTOR, JAK–STAT
Row1.Rationale: Osimertinib covalently binds EGFR C797 in the ATP pocket and is ~200‑fold more selective for mutant versus wild‑type EGFR, suppressing downstream RAS–RAF–MEK–ERK, PI3K–AKT–mTOR, and JAK–STAT cascades that drive proliferation and survival in EGFR‑mutant NSCLC.

Row2.Rationale: T790M increases ATP affinity and mediates resistance to first/second‑generation TKIs in ~50–60% of patients; osimertinib was designed to overcome T790M and is standard second‑line therapy in T790M‑positive NSCLC, restoring blockade of EGFR‑dependent downstream signaling.

Row3.Pathway ID/Name: EGFR C797S tertiary mutation and other ATP‑site mutations (G796, L718, L792, L798)
Row3.Rationale: C797S and neighboring kinase‑domain mutations (G796/C797, L718, L792, L798) disrupt covalent binding of osimertinib at C797 or alter the solvent‑front/ATP pocket, markedly increasing osimertinib IC₅₀ and allowing continued EGFR phosphorylation despite drug exposure.

Row4.Rationale: Selective amplification of mutant EGFR (including Ex19del/T790M/C797S) is observed in ~19–29% of osimertinib‑resistant tumors, enhancing oncogenic signaling and outcompeting pharmacologic inhibition, often in the absence of new tertiary mutations.

Row5.Rationale: MET amplification is among the most frequent off‑target mechanisms (≈15–24% after first‑line osimertinib; 28.6% in a real‑world cohort), reactivating ERBB3 and PI3K–AKT signaling despite EGFR blockade; dual EGFR/MET targeting with MET TKIs (savolitinib, capmatinib, tepotinib, crizotinib) or amivantamab±lazertinib can restore sensitivity.

Row6.Rationale: HER2 amplification or mutation constitutes an EGFR‑independent bypass, re‑engaging downstream MAPK and PI3K–AKT pathways under EGFR inhibition and has been reported as an acquired mechanism after first‑, second‑, and third‑generation EGFR‑TKIs including osimertinib.

Row7.Rationale: Despite proximal EGFR inhibition, resistance frequently involves reactivation of MAPK and PI3K–AKT–mTOR through MET/HER2 amplification, KRAS/NRAS/BRAF or PIK3CA mutations, or loss of PTEN; these pathways maintain proliferation and anti‑apoptotic signaling and can be targeted with MEK or mTOR/PI3K inhibitors in combination with osimertinib.

Row8.Rationale: Amplifications/mutations in CCND1/2/E1, CDK4/6 and loss of CDKN2A promote G1–S transition independent of EGFR, conferring osimertinib resistance; CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, abemaciclib) combined with osimertinib reverse resistance in preclinical models and are entering clinical testing.

Row9.Rationale: Epithelial–mesenchymal transition and transformation to small‑cell or squamous histology bypass EGFR dependence; EMT involves loss of E‑cadherin and gain of vimentin and EMT‑TFs (ZEB, TWIST), and is associated with dependence on NF‑κB and other survival pathways, limiting benefit from continued EGFR inhibition.

Row10.Rationale: In resistant patient effusions and cell models, CCL2 is upregulated; CCL2 activates STAT3 and ERK1/2, inducing ZEB1‑driven EMT and osimertinib resistance, while CCL2 knockdown or STAT3 inhibition restores sensitivity and combined STAT3 blockade plus osimertinib overcomes resistance in xenografts.

Row11.Rationale: Osimertinib‑resistant tumors show increased macrophage infiltration, M2 polarization, reduced CD8⁺ T‑cell activity, and broader immune‑microenvironmental rewiring, creating niches that protect EGFR‑mutant cells from TKIs and contribute to heterogeneous, often non‑genomic resistance.

Row12.Rationale: Multi‑model transcriptomics in EGFR‑TKI‑resistant LUAD (including osimertinib‑resistant PDCs) identify MUC16 as consistently upregulated; MUC16 knockdown re‑sensitizes resistant cells to EGFR‑TKIs, implicating a MUC16‑centered pathway (interfacing with MAPK/PI3K and adhesion programs) as a druggable vulnerability in acquired resistance.
